_meta.title: Tokio and the Async Runtime in Rust
_meta.note: Two pre-lesson and two post-lesson questions.

questions.question: What distinguishes a Tokio task from an OS thread?
options: A Tokio task is a kernel-scheduled unit of execution with its own stack.
A Tokio task is a lightweight, user-space unit of execution scheduled cooperatively by the runtime on a shared thread pool.
A Tokio task is identical to an OS thread but uses less memory.
A Tokio task runs only on a single dedicated core and cannot be migrated.
questions.explanation: Tokio tasks are user-space coroutines multiplexed onto a pool of OS threads. They have minimal per-task overhead (~tens of bytes vs ~MB per thread) and are scheduled cooperatively at .await points, not preemptively by the kernel. They can migrate between OS threads via work-stealing.

questions.question: What happens when a Tokio task calls tokio::task::yield_now().await?
options: The OS suspends the entire thread.
The task voluntarily returns Pending to the scheduler, allowing another task to run on the same worker thread.
The task is immediately moved to a different worker thread.
The task yields the CPU for exactly one scheduler tick and then resumes.
questions.explanation: yield_now() returns Pending from the current poll, which tells the scheduler to run another task. The yielding task is re-queued and will be polled again when the scheduler gets to it. This is cooperative scheduling: tasks must yield explicitly to share the worker thread.

questions.question: In Tokio's work-stealing scheduler, why is work-stealing important for performance?
options: It allows the runtime to disable the operating system's scheduler for better throughput.
It prevents thread starvation by letting idle workers steal tasks from busy workers, keeping all cores utilized.
It eliminates the need for any synchronization primitives in the runtime.
It automatically parallelizes CPU-bound tasks across GPU cores.
questions.explanation: In a multi-threaded Tokio runtime, each worker thread has its own local task queue. Under uneven load, a thread with an empty queue can "steal" tasks from another thread's queue. This keeps all worker threads busy and prevents scenarios where one thread is overloaded while others sit idle.

questions.question: When should you prefer tokio::sync::Mutex over std::sync::Mutex in an async context?
options: Always, because tokio::sync::Mutex is always faster.
When the lock guard must be held across an .await point, because std::sync::MutexGuard cannot cross .await.
Only when protecting data smaller than 64 bytes.
std::sync::Mutex should never be used in any Tokio program.
questions.explanation: std::sync::MutexGuard is not Send, so holding it across an .await would be a compile error (the guard would be held while the future is polled by a different thread). tokio::sync::Mutex's lock() is async, so it releases the lock before the .await if contention is detected, and re-acquires afterward. For short, non-.await-guarded critical sections, std::sync::Mutex is fine and faster.

questions.question: What problem does an async runtime like Tokio solve?
options: It provides garbage collection for Rust
It provides a scheduler for async tasks, an event loop for I/O, and a thread pool for CPU-bound work
It makes Rust code run on GPUs
It eliminates the need for ownership and borrowing
questions.explanation: Rust's async/await produces state machines (Futures), but something must poll them. Tokio provides: (1) a multi-threaded scheduler for tasks, (2) an I/O reactor (epoll/kqueue) for async I/O, (3) timers, (4) synchronization primitives. It's the de facto standard async runtime for Rust.
